input_candidates.truth_value: Work hardening rate peaks in the range 26–29 at.% Al, maximum at ~28% Al.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 1 shows that yield stress increases steadily with Al content up to 25% Al, followed by a rapid fall at 25–28% Al and little change or a slight increase over 30–40% Al; ductility shows an essentially inverse relationship with a rapid fall to 25% Al, a slight maximum at 28% Al, and a steady slow fall thereafter. Data are from thermally-relaxed as-cast materials with coarse grain sizes and no solute or precipitate effects.

input_candidates.truth_value: The high work hardening is explained by the combination of superdislocation dipole hardening and the predominance of straight screw dislocations due to strong Peierls (core) effects.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 2 shows work hardening rate measured as the secant over 1–1.5% plastic strain: low for disordered Fe-Al with less than 20% Al, a sharp peak in the 26–29% Al range (especially 28–29% Al), and a moderate value above about 35% Al. Similar trends are seen over strain ranges 0.5–1% and 1–2%. Two data points correspond to Fe-Al alloys containing about 0.1% B, which show little consistent change in work hardening rate relative to B-free alloys.

input_candidates.truth_value: Elastic anisotropy peaks near Fe–25–30% Al, but the authors argue that Peierls forces, not anisotropy itself, are responsible for the screw dislocation alignment and high work hardening.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 3 gives APB energies from literature: APBI in DO3 order decreases from about 78 mJ/m^2 at 25.5% Al to 65 mJ/m^2 at 28% Al; APBI in B2 order is about 65 mJ/m^2 at 31% Al rising to 110 mJ/m^2 at 36% Al; APBII in DO3 order decreases from about 78 mJ/m^2 at 26% Al to 65 mJ/m^2 at 29% Al.

input_candidates.truth_value: Addition of 0.1% B caused a slight increase in flow stress but no consistent change in work hardening rate.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 4 shows elastic stiffness constants and Zener anisotropy factor A = 2C44/(C11-C12); Fe3Al alloys exhibit extremely high elastic anisotropy due to both an increase in C44 and a fall in (C11-C12)/2.

input_candidates.truth_value: A small ductility peak at ~28% Al is attributed to the high work hardening rate meeting the Considère criterion.
input_candidates.prediction_value: The peak in work hardening rate near 28% Al is attributed to two main factors: (i) the presence of superdislocations which produce stronger superdislocation dipole locks than single dislocation dipoles, and (ii) the increased tendency of dislocations to lie in straight screw orientations due to strong Peierls forces. The coincidence with high elastic anisotropy is believed to reflect interatomic forces generating strong Peierls effects, rather than elastic anisotropy being the direct cause.

input_candidates.truth_value: Fig. 3 APB energy data from Ray–Crawford–Cockayne: APBI in DO3 order decreases from 78 to 65 mJ/m² over 25.5–28% Al, APBII 78–65 mJ/m² over 26–29% Al, APBI in B2 increases from 65 to 110 mJ/m² over 31–36% Al.
input_candidates.prediction_value: No APB tubes were observed in any deformed material, providing little support for the APB-tube-dragging model of work hardening.

input_candidates.truth_value: Fig. 4 elastic stiffness data show C44 increase and (C11-C12)/2 decrease near 25–30% Al, leading to high Zener anisotropy factor A.
input_candidates.prediction_value: No evidence of the operation of coupled four-partial dislocations (which would constitute the perfect superdislocation for the DO3 ordered state) was found in any material, including Fe-25% Al where APB energies are too low to maintain coupled superdislocations.

input_candidates.truth_value: Dislocation reactions forming sessile <100> edge dislocations were observed in Fe–28% Al and Fe–30% Al, contributing to additional hardening.
input_candidates.prediction_value: In Fe-28% Al, detailed Burgers vector analysis confirms a reaction of two superdislocations with different <111> Burgers vectors on different {110} planes producing a pair of edge <100> dislocations on a (001) plane, e.g., [111] + [11-1] -> [100] edge along [010], constituting sessile locks that prevent further room temperature superdislocation motion.
